content:

# OpenClaw x Discord Server Integration Guide. Architecture diagram of OpenClaw integrated with Discord server. After embedding an OpenClaw AI agent into a Discord server, your community gains the following capabilities:. Compared to Telegram's personal remote control scenario or Slack's enterprise workflow scenario, the Discord x OpenClaw integration is best suited for **community management, open-source project support, and large-scale multi-user interaction**. Step 1: Create a Discord Bot and Obtain Token. This is the foundation of the entire **OpenClaw Discord setup** process -- creating an Application and Bot on the Discord Developer Portal and obtaining the necessary authentication Token. ### 3.1 Create a Discord Application. ### 3.2 Create Bot and Obtain Token. **Important security reminder:** The Bot Token is equivalent to full access credentials for the Bot -- anyone who has it can control your Bot. Never expose the Token in public places (GitHub, Discord channels, screenshots). After obtaining the Discord Bot Token, the next step is to write it into OpenClaw's configuration so the Gateway can connect to Discord as your Bot.

title: 🦞 Discord - OpenClaw
content:
OpenClaw 技术干货

# Discord. OpenClaw connects to Discord as a bot over the official Discord gateway. DMs and guild channels are supported. Discord DMs default to pairing mode. Create a Discord application with a bot, add the bot to your server, and pair it with OpenClaw. Use a private server if you can; create one first (**Create My Own > For me and my friends**) if needed. ### Create a Discord application and bot. In the Discord Developer Portal, click **New Application** and name it (for example "OpenClaw"). Open **Bot** in the sidebar and set the **Username** to your agent's name. On the **Bot** page, click **Reset Token** and copy the token. Keep the Server ID and User ID with your bot token; you need all three next. If your host is blocked or rate-limited by Discord's startup application lookup, set the application/client ID from the Developer Portal so startup can skip that REST call: `channels.discord.applicationId` for the default account, or `channels.discord.accounts.<accountId>.applicationId` per bot.

There was an error while loading. * Notifications  You must be signed in to change notification settings. # [Feature]: Add allowBots support for Telegram groups (parity with Discord/Slack) #8295. enhancementNew feature or requestNew feature or request. ## Description. opened on Feb 3, 2026. Discord and Slack providers already support allowBots to enable bot-to-bot communication in channels/groups. Telegram groups don't have this option, which makes multi-bot setups (e.g. two MoltBot instances in the same Telegram group) unable to interact directly. Use case: Two MoltBot agents in a shared Telegram group, each serving a different user, able to respond to each other's messages when mentioned. ## Metadata. No fields configured for issues without a type. No branches or pull requests. You can’t perform that action at this time.

title: 🦞 [RFC] Event-driven cron scheduling: job chaining and reactive triggers · Issue #28584 · openclaw/openclaw · GitHub
content:
OpenClaw 技术干货

You signed in with another tab or window. # [RFC] Event-driven cron scheduling: job chaining and reactive triggers #28584. Add a new schedule type to the cron system that lets jobs trigger based on **completion of other jobs** rather than time:. * **V1 (this RFC):** `schedule.kind='after'` — run Job B when Job A completes (success/failure/any). * **Future (separate RFC):** `schedule.kind='event'` — run a job when an internal event fires (e.g., `cron:completed`, `message:received`). Today, every cron job is independent — there's no way to express "run this after that finishes.". * A job can depend on only one parent (no fan-in for V1). **Contention handling:** If the dependent job is already running when the parent completes (e.g., from a previous trigger or a parallel cron schedule), the chained trigger should **queue** the run rather than immediately executing. **Pipeline context:** The parent run can pass a small context payload to the child via a `triggerContext` field on the run record.

title: 🦞 OpenClaw Cron Scheduled Tasks Guide | MI
content:
OpenClaw 技术干货

# OpenClaw Cron Scheduled Tasks Guide. Diagram of OpenClaw Cron scheduled task automation execution. What Is OpenClaw's Cron? If you're familiar with Linux's `crontab`, OpenClaw's Cron is conceptually similar -- it automatically executes tasks at specified times. But the key difference is: traditional crontab executes fixed Shell commands, while OpenClaw's Cron executes **natural language instructions**, with the AI agent autonomously deciding how to complete them. `"Every day at 9 AM, check server status and send a summary to Telegram"`. The latter doesn't require you to write scripts in advance -- the agent will decide which check commands to execute, how to organize the results, and how to format the report on its own. `openclaw cron add --schedule "0 9 * * *" --message "Check the server's CPU and memory usage. If a Cron task's execution time exceeds this value, the Gateway will forcibly terminate the connection, causing the task to be truncated -- the agent may have completed 80% of the work, but cannot return results due to the timeout.

# OpenClaw cron scheduler guide with main and isolated runs. OpenClaw cron scheduler guide with main and isolated runs - OpenClaw cron scheduler guide with main and isolated runs. That’s what the built-in cron scheduler is for. It runs inside the Gateway process so you don’t need a separate job runner and it can deliver results straight back to Telegram WhatsApp Slack Discord and more. This isn’t just “run a script at 7 AM.” It’s “wake the agent run a prompt or drop a system event into the main session then deliver output where I will actually see it.”. OpenClaw cron is a scheduler that lives inside the Gateway. You can send the same job output to a private Telegram chat and also to a Slack channel if you want. Another one is a weekly review that runs in a clean isolated session and only posts the final result back to you.

title: 🦞 OpenClaw: Scheduled tasks
content:
OpenClaw 技术干货

Cron is the Gateway's built-in scheduler. It persists jobs, wakes the agent at the right time, and can deliver output to a chat channel, a webhook, or nowhere. `openclaw cron create "2027-02-01T16:00:00Z" \ --name "Reminder" \ --session main \ --system-event "Reminder: check the cron docs draft" \ --wake now \ --delete-after-run`. `openclaw cron listopenclaw cron get <job-id>openclaw cron show <job-id>`. `openclaw cron runs --id <job-id>`. Cron task reconciliation is runtime-owned first, durable-history-backed second: an active cron task stays live while the cron runtime still tracks that job as running, even if an old child session row still exists. Once the runtime stops owning the job and a 5-minute grace window expires, maintenance checks persisted run logs and job state for the matching `cron:<jobId>:<startedAt>` run. To require both conditions, use croner's `+` day-of-week modifier (`0 9 15 * +1`), or schedule on one field and guard the other in your job's prompt or command. On `cron edit`, removes the per-job model override so the job follows normal cron model precedence (stored cron-session override, else agent/default model).

title: 🦞 Cron Jobs & Sub-Agents - Introduction to OpenClaw | Stanza
content:
OpenClaw 技术干货

# Cron Jobs & Sub-Agents. These features let you schedule recurring tasks and spawn child sessions that communicate with each other, enabling sophisticated automation workflows. `// Creating a cron job using the cron tool. // The job is persisted with key: cron:dependency-check`. The `cron.create` call registers a job with a unique `id`, a standard cron `schedule` expression, the `command` to execute, and any `context` the job needs. The job is stored under the key `cron:dependency-check` and will persist across sessions, meaning it continues to run even after you close and reopen OpenClaw. // Returns: [{ id: 'dependency-check', schedule: '0 * * * *', ... const job = await cron.get('dependency-check');. Cron jobs can run in two modes: in the **main session** (where the job's output is added to the next heartbeat turn) or in an **isolated session** (where the job gets its own fresh context window). `// A cron job that spawns a sub-agent for nightly analysis.
